item.title: Honda and the power of (broken) dreams
item.description: A brilliant and adventurist beacon for Japanese industry in the past is now stumbling

item.title: King Charles and the ‘special’ relationship
item.description: Ties with the US need mending, but Britain must nurture other alliances too

item.title: Meta’s Chinese stumble suggests a declining tolerance for shades of grey
item.description: Tech-related capital flows have benefited from decades of ambiguity, but AI changes the calculus

item.title: Why London’s teens need more third spaces
item.description: At a time when young people are either demonised for staying in or going out, youth clubs are more important than ever

item.title: The EU’s methane regulation could spark an energy crisis
item.description: Its extraterritorial, prescriptive and paperwork-heavy requirements will restrict access to supply options

item.title: Why flying less won’t do much to ease the oil crisis
item.description: Crude demand weakens when not only jet fuel but other product prices rise too

item.title: Are Europe and America headed for divorce?
item.description: The US military commitment to Europe is fraying — but the two sides remain locked in an unhappy marriage for now

item.title: This rally runs deep
item.description: Plus, the global won

item.title: Unequal Britain can’t work out what to do about it
item.description: Regional disparities in health, wealth and education are a blight on our economy and society

item.title: Can London win back home buyers from the UAE?
item.description: The effect of the Middle East war on Dubai’s property market has been instant. But better financial incentives are needed for wealthy expats to choose the capital over Monaco or Geneva

item.title: Healthcare is driving America’s economy
item.description: This is a huge industry — and that’s not necessarily a good thing

item.title: Help! Our newest client is an AI model
item.description: A behind-the-scenes look at the work of Rutherford Hall, critical communications strategist

item.title: Trump and the recurring assassin
item.description: Saturday evening’s shooting was the third assassination attempt on the US president in less than two years

item.title: Starbucks, Nashville and the new corporate geography
item.description: Big US companies are realising they can unbundle their headquarters

item.title: Britain acts richer than it is
item.description: The country’s habits and virtues are built for a prosperity it no longer enjoys

item.title: The Iran war shock for developing nations
item.description: Poorer countries face a triple whammy of crises in fuel, food and remittances

item.title: Why bank taxes might be back on the table in the UK
item.description: The problem with ‘windfall’ taxes is that the relatively small amount raised is often not worth the long-term cost

item.title: Can AI discriminate if it can’t justify itself?
item.description: Elon Musk’s lawsuit against Colorado raises a deeper philosophical question about artificial intelligence and democracy

item.title: This World Cup is missing an open goal
item.description: And I should know — as Mexican ambassador to the US, I helped to craft the original bid

item.title: Good news for business travellers
item.description: The seats in economy that so many of us use are finally getting an upgrade

item.title: Chart of the Week: The S&P just doesn’t care
item.description: The war is over — in the US stock market, at least

item.title: Jancis Robinson’s A-Z of 21st-century wine
item.description: A guide to what’s new in the wine world

item.title: The mystery of good judgment
item.description: It does more to shape a life than intellect and hard work

item.title: Power is draining away from Starmer’s Downing Street
item.description: Loyalty has worn thin because of the PM’s lack of interest in making the difficult decisions his job requires

item.title: What the AI ‘jobpocalypse’ narrative misses
item.description: Whether or not new technologies can perform a task is only a tiny part of the picture
